mnemonics: Trees are data structures where each node has a maximum of two children (left child and right child).
A binary tree is a tree data structure in which each node has a maximum of two children.
The root of a binary tree is the topmost node. Think 'Root Of Everything'.
In a binary tree, all the nodes are arranged in a way that for each node, the left child is less than the parent node and the right child is greater than the parent node.
Recursion is used to traverse binary trees. Think 'Recursive Child'.
Recursion is used to traverse the left subtree and the right subtree. Think 'Recursive Child, Recursive Child'.
Recursion is used to traverse the left subtree, the right subtree, and then the current node. Think 'Recursive Child, Recursive Child, Current Node'.
Recursion is used to traverse the current node and then the left subtree, and then the right subtree. Think 'Current Node, Recursive Child, Recursive Child'.
Recursion is used to traverse the current node and then the right subtree, and then the left subtree. Think 'Current Node, Recursive Child, Recursive Child'.
Recursion is used to traverse the left subtree, the right subtree, and then the current node. Think 'Recursive Child, Recursive Child, Current Node'.
The binary tree is traversed using pre-order, in-order, post-order, etc. Think 'Pre-Order, In-Order, Post-Order'.
The binary tree is traversed in the order of root, left child, right child. Think 'Root, Left Child, Right Child'.
keyPoints: 10.1: Definition of binary trees and their properties
10.2: Types of binary trees (perfect, incomplete, full)
10.5: Traversal of binary trees (pre-order, in-order, post-order)
10.5.3.1: Post-order traversal of the left subtree
10.5.3.2: Post-order traversal of the right subtree
10.5.3.4: Post-order traversal of the current node
